rulings.Case Title: EVEREST NATIONAL INSURANCE COMPANY VS. HMI ENTERPRISES INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, June 14, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANT DELAPLACE INSURANCE'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendant Delaplace Insurance's motion for summary judgment is GRANTED. Delaplace owes no duty to Plaintiff Everest National Insurance Company. An insurance broker has no duty to a third party to provide coverage to the broker's clients. (Travelers Property Casualty Company of America v. Superior Court (2013) 215 Cal.App.4th 561, 565.) The third party's remedy is against the client for failing in its obligation to procure insurance, not against the broker for failing to provide it. (Id.) The Subcontract Agreement requires the subcontractor, HMI Enterprises, Inc., to obtain workers compensation insurance, but does not mention Delaplace or place any contractual obligation upon it. Furthermore, unlike the plaintiff in Business to Business Markets v. Zurich Specialties (2005) 135 Cal.App.4th 165, neither Terra Nova nor Everest are intended beneficiaries of the Agreement. Plaintiff stands in the shoes of Terra Nova, its insured. (Amco Ins. Company v. All Solutions Ins. Agency, LLC (2016) 244 Cal.App.4th 883, 886.) But Terra Nova would not receive funds under a workers compensation insurance policy; it was merely obligated to pay when HMI's employee, Laureano Madrigal, was injured on the job and HMI discovered it was uninsured and did not pay the claim. (See Business to Business, 135 Cal.App.4th at 168-169 [explaining that Business to Business Markets was intended to be the beneficiary of the insurance and receive funds directly in case of Tricon's breach].) Moreover, decisions that have followed Business to Business have clarified that the Biakanja factors relied upon in that decision are inapplicable when claims are based in negligence and not in breach of contract. (See, e.g., Pacific Rim Mechanical Contractors, Inc. v. Aon Risk Ins. Services West Inc. (2012) 203 Cal.App.4th 1278, 1291-1292.) Thus, there is no basis here to rely upon those factors to impose a duty on Delaplace where its relationship with Plaintiff was only incidental to the Agreement and where Plaintiff is suing Delaplace for negligence. With regard to Plaintiff's fraud claim, Plaintiff is unable to demonstrate that it reasonably relied upon the alleged misrepresentation by Delaplace because the Certificate of Insurance (COI) is dated August 15, 2014. (Delaplace's Exh. C) Terra Nova could not have relied on the COI to enter into a contract with HMI because the Subcontract Agreement is dated August 8, 2014, a week before the date of the COI, and the accident in which Madrigal was injured occurred on August 12, 2014, three days before the date of the COI. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Delaplace is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: FORCE OF NATURE, INC. ET AL VS. SHEA LABAGH DOBBERSTEIN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Sanctions For Defendants Spoliation Of Evidence
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, June 14, 2019, Line 3, PLAINTIFFs FORCE OF NATURE, INC., and PETER KLAPPER's Motion For Sanctions For Defendants Spoliation Of Evidence Pro Tem Judge Greg Groeneveld, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Parties to appear. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to greg@greggroeneveldlaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ZAHIRA SALMA VS. ARRIBA JUNTOS
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Arriba Juntos Motion To Dismiss For Lack Of Subject Matter Jurisdiction (For Tentatiover Purposes Only Par 2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, June 17, 2019, Line 10, Defendant Arriba Juntos Motion To Dismiss For Lack Of Subject Matter Jurisdiction (part 1 of 2) Defendant Arriba Juntos's motion to dismiss for lack of subject matter jurisdiction is denied. First, Defendant waived the protection of the workers' compensation statute by failing to plead it as an affirmative defense in its answer. "Generally speaking, in a lawsuit for personal injury damages, a defendant has the burden of pleading and proving, as an affirmative defense, the existence of the conditions of compensation under the workers' compensation law." (Brown v. Desert Christian Center (2011) 193 Cal.App.4th 733, 739, citing Doney v. Tambouratgis (1979) 23 Cal.3d 91, 96.) There may be exceptions to the rule, such as where the applicability of the rule is raised by the allegations of the complaint or by evidence submitted with a motion for summary judgment. (See Rowland v. County of Sonoma (1990) 220 Cal.App.3d 331, 334 [exceptions exist if the complaint directly or indirectly alleges facts indicating an employment relationship or when postpleading motions indicate that the conditions of compensation might exist]; cf. Lucich v. City of Oakland (1993) 19 Cal.App.4th 494, 499 [limiting Rowland to its "unique facts" and characterizing its discussion as dicta].) However, none applies here. Second, even assuming that Defendant had not waived the defense and that Plaintiff Zahia Salma was an employee of Arriba Juntos (which she disputes), Defendant has failed to show that Plaintiff's injury was sustained in the course of her employment and arose out of her employment. (Lab. Code § 3600.) Plaintiff was injured as a result of slipping and falling on the stairs of Defendant's building (which was not her workplace) while picking up her paycheck. A later claim for workers' compensation benefits that Defendant apparently submitted on her behalf after this action was filed was denied on the ground that "there is no substantial medical, factual or legal evidence to indicate [she] sustained an injury while employed with Arriba Juntos while insured with Cypress Insurance Company." (Ahumada Decl., Ex. E.) Robbins v. Yellow Cab Co. (1948) 85 Cal.App.2d 811, is closely on point. There, as here, plaintiff was injured when she slipped and fell on her employer's premises when picking up a paycheck (there, her husband's paycheck) outside her normal work hours. The court agreed that because plaintiff "was neither at work nor doing any act that could fairly be construed as within the scope of her employment or as an aid to her employer," workers compensation exclusivity did not apply. (Id. at 813.) The court explained, "For the injury to occur in the 'course of the employment' the employee must be engaged in the work he has been hired to perform; it must occur during within the period of his employment and at a place where he may reasonably be for that purpose while engaged in the performance of his duties or while doing something necessarily incident thereto." (Id.) "The voluntary visit of an employee to the premises of his employer for the purpose of attending to a strictly personal matter is not such an act s the employee might reasonably be expected to do in the performance of his duties." (Id.) Further, the workers' compensation law "excludes an injury which comes from a hazard to which the workman would be equally exposed apart from the employment." (Id. at 814.) =(302/RBU)
